html:
Acrylic Coatings for Metal and Well-Draining Roofs

Acrylic roof coating is our go-to recommendation for extending the life of metal roofs across Shreveport-Bossier's I-20 warehouse and manufacturing stock, and for well-draining single-ply or built-up roofs that don't have chronic ponding water problems. It costs less than silicone and performs well on the roof types where standing water isn't a factor.

Where Acrylic Makes More Sense Than Silicone

Acrylic coating's one real limitation is tolerance for standing water — it can soften and degrade in areas where water sits for extended periods. On a roof with good drainage and no known low spots, that limitation doesn't matter, and acrylic gives you strong UV reflectivity and weathering protection at a lower cost than silicone. Most of the R-panel and standing seam metal roofs we recoat across this market fall into exactly that category: sloped enough to shed water, so acrylic is the more budget-friendly right answer.

Rust Encapsulation on Older Metal Roofs

A lot of the metal roofing out along I-20 and through the surrounding industrial parishes has been up for twenty or thirty years, and surface rust is common at fastener heads, cut edges, and scratched panel coating. Acrylic coating systems can encapsulate light surface rust as part of the recoat process, sealing it from further moisture exposure and stopping the corrosion cycle rather than just painting over it cosmetically. We check corrosion depth during inspection, because rust that's progressed past surface level needs panel repair or replacement before coating, not coating over it.

Reflectivity and Cooling Load on Metal Buildings

Bare or aged metal roofing absorbs a lot of solar heat through a Louisiana summer, and a reflective acrylic topcoat can cut rooftop surface temperature significantly compared to an unpainted or faded panel finish. For manufacturing and warehouse buildings running rooftop-mounted HVAC or refrigeration, that reduction translates into real, measurable relief on equipment that's otherwise working overtime through July and August heat.

What a Metal Roof Recoat Project Includes

  • Fastener inspection and replacement of any failed washers before coating
  • Pressure washing to remove chalking, dirt, and biological growth from the panel surface
  • Rust treatment and encapsulation at affected fastener heads and panel edges
  • Seam and lap sealing at panel joints and flashing transitions
  • Acrylic base coat and topcoat application at manufacturer-specified thickness

Skipping the fastener check before coating is a common shortcut that shows up as new leaks within a year or two, since a coating applied over already-loose fasteners doesn't stop the underlying backout problem.

Recoat Cycle and What to Budget For

Acrylic coatings typically need a fresh topcoat on a shorter cycle than silicone, since the material doesn't hold up quite as long under sustained UV exposure. Budgeting for that recoat cycle as routine maintenance, rather than an unplanned emergency line item, keeps the roof performing and avoids the gap where a coating has worn thin enough to let leaks start again.

Which Buildings Should Skip Acrylic Entirely

If your roof has a known low spot where water sits for more than a day after rain, acrylic is the wrong coating regardless of how much cheaper it is than silicone. We see this mistake occasionally on buildings where a previous contractor recoated with acrylic across a whole roof field without accounting for a ponding area, and the coating in that one spot fails years ahead of the rest of the field. Rather than coat over that risk, we'll either recommend silicone for the whole roof or silicone specifically at the ponding areas with acrylic elsewhere, depending on what makes sense for your budget.

The distribution and manufacturing buildings along I-20 tend to have large, unbroken metal roof fields with minimal shading, which means they take the full brunt of Louisiana summer sun for a long stretch of the day. A fresh acrylic topcoat on one of these roofs makes a visible difference in rooftop surface temperature, and for a building with rooftop-mounted refrigeration or process equipment sensitive to ambient heat, that reflectivity gain is often the most cost-effective heat-load improvement available for the roof. A metal roof that's properly maintained and recoated on this kind of schedule can go through several acrylic recoat cycles over its life before panel replacement ever becomes necessary, and each cycle is cheaper than the last major repair would have been if the coating had been allowed to fully fail first.

Common Questions About Acrylic Roof Coatings

Is acrylic a good fit for our metal warehouse roof?

In most cases, yes, provided the roof drains well and doesn't have chronic ponding. It's a cost-effective way to extend service life and improve reflectivity.

Can acrylic coating fix surface rust on our panels?

Light surface rust can be encapsulated as part of the coating process. Deeper corrosion needs panel repair before we coat over it.

Why would we choose acrylic over silicone?

Cost, mainly — acrylic is less expensive and performs well on roofs without standing water problems. Silicone is the better call where ponding is a factor.

How long does an acrylic coating typically last before recoating?

It varies with exposure and film thickness, but generally on a shorter cycle than silicone, which is worth budgeting into routine maintenance.

Does coating address fastener backout on R-panel roofs?

Not on its own — we inspect and repair fasteners as part of the prep work before coating, since a coating applied over loose fasteners won't stop the underlying problem.
